name: Collaborative 3D Editor
oneLiner: A real-time collaborative 3D scene editor for the web, with live multi-user sync, persistence, and interactive object editing.
description: A full-stack web application where multiple users can create and edit the same 3D scene in real time. The project combines a React-based 3D editor with an ASP.NET Core backend, SignalR-powered realtime synchronization, and PostgreSQL persistence. It was built to explore shared state, concurrent edits, live presence, and the challenges of making collaborative tooling feel responsive and reliable in the browser.
features: multi-user scene editing
real-time object synchronization
live collaborator presence
live selection indicators
scene persistence with PostgreSQL
3D object creation, transform, and material editing
undo and redo support
scene dashboard with rendered thumbnails
responsive dashboard and mobile-friendly editor layout
problemsSolved: handling concurrent updates in a shared realtime scene
keeping client state and persisted database state consistent
broadcasting collaborative presence and selection without desynchronizing users
designing a clean API split between REST endpoints and realtime SignalR events
making interactive 3D editing usable inside a browser-based interface
deploying a full-stack realtime application with database, API, and frontend hosting

architecture.frontend: React + TypeScript application using React Three Fiber/Three.js for the 3D editor UI
architecture.backend: ASP.NET Core Web API with SignalR hub for realtime scene updates
architecture.database: PostgreSQL with Entity Framework Core for scene and object persistence
architecture.deployment: Frontend and backend deployed separately, with PostgreSQL hosted as a managed service

awards: Best project award in the course Webbprogrammering 2
recruiterTalkingPoints: Built a full-stack collaborative product rather than a static demo
Implemented realtime multi-user synchronization with persistent storage
Worked across frontend interaction design, backend architecture, database modeling, and deployment
Focused on both technical depth and product polish, including thumbnails, undo/redo, and responsive layout

name: Proxy Inspector
oneLiner: A custom HTTP forward proxy with rule-based traffic control, a monitoring REST API, and a live developer dashboard.
description: A full-stack developer tool built to inspect and monitor HTTP traffic flowing through a custom proxy. The backend is written in Java and handles request parsing, policy evaluation, forwarding, transaction logging, and API exposure without relying on heavyweight frameworks. On top of that, the project includes a React and TypeScript dashboard that visualizes traffic health, request stats, verdicts, durations, bytes relayed, and recent transaction history in real time. The project was built to explore low-level HTTP handling, policy enforcement, backend design, and how to present operational data through a clean monitoring interface.
features: custom HTTP forward proxy
host blocking rules
host-specific path blocking
per-client rate limiting
transaction logging with verdicts, durations, and bytes relayed
REST API for health, stats, and transaction history
live monitoring dashboard with health badge and overview cards
transaction table with verdict filtering, limit selection, and auto-refresh
relative timestamps and clear loading, empty, and error states
backend unit tests for config parsing, policy logic, transaction aggregation, and request parsing
problemsSolved: building a forward proxy from lower-level Java networking primitives instead of relying on a proxy framework
separating proxy behavior, policy evaluation, transaction storage, and API exposure into a clean architecture
tracking and surfacing useful operational data such as blocked requests, errors, durations, and bytes transferred
designing a frontend that makes raw proxy traffic understandable at a glance
connecting a custom backend API to a responsive dashboard with live polling and filtering
documenting and packaging a full-stack developer tool as a polished portfolio project

architecture.frontend: React and TypeScript single-page dashboard built with Vite, polling the backend API for health, stats, and recent transactions
architecture.backend: Java 17 application with a custom HTTP forward proxy, policy engine, transaction store, and lightweight REST API server
architecture.database: No database; transactions are stored in memory with a configurable retention limit
architecture.deployment: Designed primarily for local development and portfolio demonstration, with the frontend and backend running as separate processes

awards: Best project award in the course Programming for Internet
recruiterTalkingPoints: Built custom backend infrastructure instead of only consuming existing services or frameworks
Worked across low-level networking, API design, frontend monitoring UX, and project documentation
Turned a systems-style backend project into a polished full-stack portfolio piece with a live dashboard
Added tests around the core backend logic to make the project more credible and maintainable
Demonstrates both technical depth and product thinking through observability, filtering, and developer-facing UX

name: GitHub Security Scanner
oneLiner: A full-stack GitHub repository security scanning workspace with queued execution, findings storage, and report review.
description: A full-stack web application for scanning GitHub repositories and reviewing security findings in a focused workspace. The project combines a React frontend, an Express and Prisma backend, GitHub OAuth plus GitHub App integration, PostgreSQL persistence, Redis-backed queue processing, and a Strix-powered scan pipeline. It was built to explore repository synchronization, async job orchestration, scan lifecycle handling, cancellation and recovery flows, and how to present security analysis in a clear product interface.
features: GitHub OAuth login flow
GitHub App installation and repository sync
repository picker populated from connected GitHub installations
queued scan execution with BullMQ and Redis
scan lifecycle states including pending, running, completed, failed, and cancelled
scan cancellation and interrupted-run recovery
persisted findings and report storage in PostgreSQL
security workspace for reviewing reports and vulnerabilities
repository metadata display including visibility, branch, language, and latest commit
structured vulnerability presentation with severity, description, and remediation guidance
problemsSolved: connecting GitHub authentication and GitHub App installation into one coherent workflow
syncing repository metadata from GitHub into a local relational data model
running long-lived scan jobs asynchronously without blocking the request cycle
handling stuck cancellations and recovering scans left in inconsistent states after restarts
persisting scan results in a way that supports both lifecycle tracking and findings review
designing a UI that makes scan progress, report output, and findings easy to understand quickly

architecture.frontend: React and TypeScript application with authenticated routes, a repository-driven scan workspace, and findings/report views
architecture.backend: Express API with GitHub OAuth, GitHub App integration, scan endpoints, queue orchestration, and Strix process execution
architecture.database: PostgreSQL via Prisma for users, installations, repositories, scans, reports, and vulnerabilities
architecture.infrastructure: Redis for BullMQ job processing, Docker Compose for local services, and ngrok for GitHub callback and webhook tunneling during development

recruiterTalkingPoints: Built a full-stack product around a real GitHub integration rather than a static frontend concept
Worked across authentication, API design, background jobs, persistence, and UI workflow design
Handled real engineering issues like cancellation bugs, queue failures, and recovery of interrupted jobs
Integrated third-party tooling into a usable product flow instead of treating it as an isolated script
Focused on both technical depth and product clarity by shaping the app into a clean security workspace

name: StudySphere
oneLiner: An AI-assisted study workspace for organizing lecture material, generating summaries and quizzes, and planning study sessions.
description: A full-stack study app built around the real student workflow of collecting lecture material, reviewing it, practicing it, and planning when to revisit it. Users can create courses, add lectures from document/PDF links, read the source material inside the app, generate AI summaries, generate multiple-choice quizzes from lecture content, and manage study sessions in a built-in calendar. The project combines a React frontend, a TypeScript/Express backend, OpenAI-powered content generation, Prisma/PostgreSQL data storage, and a polished interface designed to feel like a modern learning product.
features: course and lecture organization
lecture creation from document and PDF URLs
inline lecture reading workflow
AI-generated lecture summaries
AI-generated multiple-choice quizzes from lecture content
day, week, and month calendar views
study session and event creation
homepage dashboard with today's events and upcoming events
dark mode by default with light mode toggle
responsive learning-app style interface
problemsSolved: turning long lecture PDFs into faster-to-review study material
generating useful quiz questions from unstructured lecture content
keeping lecture content, summaries, quizzes, and planning in one workflow
designing a frontend experience that feels polished across dark and light mode
structuring a full-stack app around course, lecture, and study-session relationships
running the app locally with a containerized PostgreSQL-backed development setup

architecture.frontend: React + TypeScript SPA built with Vite, using Tailwind CSS for styling and TipTap for rich summary display/editing
architecture.backend: Express + TypeScript API handling authentication, course and lecture management, PDF/content extraction, and AI-powered summary and quiz generation
architecture.database: PostgreSQL with Prisma for users, courses, lectures, and related application data
architecture.deployment: Primarily configured for local full-stack development with Docker Compose for the backend and database

recruiterTalkingPoints: Built a full-stack product with real user flows instead of a single isolated AI feature
Integrated OpenAI into a practical education workflow by generating summaries and quiz content from lecture material
Worked across frontend UX, backend API design, database modeling, and local Docker-based infrastructure
Focused on product polish as well as functionality, including theming, dashboard flows, modals, calendar planning, and lecture workspace design

name: Interactive Portfolio Platform
oneLiner: A 3D portfolio experience with custom screen-based navigation, a backend knowledge system, and an AI assistant grounded in project-specific data.
description: A full-stack portfolio platform built to present projects through a more interactive and technically differentiated experience than a standard website. The frontend combines React, React Three Fiber, Three.js, and layered HTML overlays to create screen-based navigation, animated transitions, and project-specific presentation flows. The backend includes an Express API, a structured knowledge layer, and an OpenAI-powered assistant that answers questions using retrieved portfolio data rather than a generic chatbot approach. The project was built to explore interactive UI architecture, grounded AI responses, and how to turn a portfolio into a product-like system.
features: 3D screen-based portfolio navigation
custom overlays for project, about, and chat views
interactive project presentation flows
AI chat assistant grounded in portfolio knowledge
structured backend knowledge retrieval for projects, skills, and experience
persistent chat history tied to a client-specific user ID
responsive layout combining WebGL scenes with HTML content
custom visual polish including animated transitions, layered depth, and subtle motion
problemsSolved: making a portfolio feel like a product experience instead of a static content site
combining 3D rendering and conventional HTML overlays without creating fragile interaction layers
keeping assistant responses grounded in curated portfolio data rather than hallucinated generic answers
structuring portfolio content into retrievable knowledge chunks for better question answering
designing a UI that balances visual personality with readability and professional clarity
coordinating frontend presentation, backend APIs, and AI integration as one coherent system

architecture.frontend: React + TypeScript application using React Three Fiber and Three.js for the 3D scene layer, with HTML overlays for content-heavy screens and interaction flows
architecture.backend: Express + TypeScript API handling chat requests, knowledge retrieval, request validation, and assistant orchestration
architecture.database: PostgreSQL with Prisma for persisted chat threads and messages
architecture.deployment: Frontend and backend deployed as separate services, with the assistant grounded in structured local portfolio data

recruiterTalkingPoints: Built a portfolio as a real interactive product rather than a static marketing page
Combined 3D frontend work, backend API design, knowledge retrieval, and AI integration in one project
Focused on grounded assistant behavior by structuring portfolio data instead of relying on an unbounded chatbot
Demonstrates both visual product thinking and systems-level engineering across the full stack
